name: Poem: "Advice from a Tree" — Ilan Shamir

sourceRefs.label: civilserviceaspirants.in - dedicated MCQ set, idiom-meaning focused
civilserviceaspirants.in dedicated MCQ page (questions 7-12) — additional idiom/phrase-meaning questions on later lines of the poem
billjohnsononline.com / tumblr.com (woodlandtrust) — full poem text, used to identify and cover lines not yet tested by the original 6-question pool

researchNotes: OCR index search for 'advice from a tree'/'ilan shamir' returned zero hits — web research only. Raw pool of 6 sourced MCQs, all kept — note this poem's question style leans heavily on idiom/phrase MEANING ('go out on a limb', 'stand tall and proud') rather than figure-of-speech labelling, a different testing emphasis than most other poems covered so far. Expanded 2026-07 pass: pulled 6 more sourced questions (7-12) from the same coaching-site MCQ set covering later lines of the poem, plus one authored QA mapping the four seasons to their meanings, since the poem's own season stanza is a natural additional angle beyond just the Fall/'letting go' line already covered.

reading.intro: A poem using a tree's life as an extended metaphor for life advice — each tree-related image (roots, limbs, seasons, leaves) maps to a piece of wisdom. Unlike most other poems in this syllabus, real exam evidence here favours idiom/phrase MEANING questions over figure-of-speech labelling — know what each phrase means, not just what device it is.

qa.question: Who wrote "Advice from a Tree", and what's notable about him?
qa.answer: Ilan Shamir — notable for having planted over 50,000 trees and led more than 800 tree-planting celebrations worldwide, directly living out the nature-connection theme of his own poem. (He also wrote "The River", elsewhere in this same syllabus.)

qa.question: What is the poem's central theme?
qa.answer: Nature as a guide for living — using a tree's life and growth as a metaphor for how to live well.

qa.question: What does "Stand tall and proud" mean in the poem?
qa.answer: Have confidence and self-respect — not arrogance or literal height, but quiet inner confidence.

qa.question: What does "Sink your roots deeply into the Earth" mean?
qa.answer: Stay grounded and stable — having a firm foundation/values to rely on, just as a tree's roots anchor and stabilize it.

qa.question: What does "Go out on a limb" mean?
qa.answer: Take a risk — a common English idiom (literally, climbing onto a tree branch that might not hold your weight) used here for stepping beyond your comfort zone.

qa.question: What do the poem's seasons represent, and what does "the wisdom to let go of leaves in the Fall" symbolize?
qa.answer: The seasons represent stages and lessons of life. Letting go of leaves in Fall symbolizes letting go of burdens or attachments — knowing when to release what no longer serves you, just as a tree sheds leaves before winter.

qa.question: Real exam-style: What figure of speech appears in "Let your limbs sway and dance in the breezes"?
qa.answer: Personification — a tree's limbs are given the human ability to 'dance', a human/lively action attributed to a plant.

qa.question: Real exam-style: What does "Embrace with joy the changing seasons" encourage?
qa.answer: Accepting change in life — welcoming life's different phases with joy rather than resisting them, just as a tree doesn't resist the changing seasons.

qa.question: Real exam-style: What does "Reflect the light of a greater source" mean?
qa.answer: Share wisdom and goodness that comes from a higher power/greater source — not literally about mirrors or torches, but about channeling and passing on light/goodness you've received.

qa.question: Real exam-style: What does "Remember your roots" suggest?
qa.answer: Stay connected to your origin and values — don't forget where you came from, just as a tree's roots remain its permanent foundation even as it grows tall.

qa.question: Real exam-style: What do "Earth, fresh air, light" (described as 'simple pleasures') symbolize in the poem?
qa.answer: The basic joys and nourishment in life — the poem's argument that true contentment comes from simple, freely-available things, not elaborate luxuries.

qa.question: What quality does each of the four seasons represent in the poem's own words: Spring, Summer, Fall, and Winter?
qa.answer: Spring = 'The Energy and Birth'; Summer = 'The Growth and Contentment'; Fall = 'The Wisdom to let go of leaves'; Winter = 'The Rest and Quiet Renewal' — together mapping a full life-cycle of growth, fulfilment, release, and rest onto the calendar year.
qa.tip: Memorize the pairing in order (Spring-Energy/Birth, Summer-Growth/Contentment, Fall-Wisdom/Letting go, Winter-Rest/Renewal) — TNPSC could ask about any single season in isolation.

qa.question: Is "Advice from a Tree" written in a fixed rhyme scheme, and how is it structured overall?
qa.answer: No — it is written in free verse (no fixed rhyme scheme or meter), structured as a direct-address letter-style poem, opening with 'Dear Friend' and delivering a series of imperative (command-form) pieces of advice, as if the tree itself is speaking to the reader.
